coredata.dc:description: Water is an essential component of life on Earth. This “simple” substance composed of hydrogen and oxygen is mandatory for the survival of human beings. Despite this, the availability of drinking water as well as water sources has been a great concern since the beginning of the 19th century. The number of water contaminants has increased over time, causing enough pollution to restrict the use of the water sources worldwide. Some examples include heavy metals and nanoparticles as inorganic contaminants, and pharmaceuticals and explosives as organic contaminants. Taking this into account, there is an increasing demand for different nanosystems to perform the detection, quantification, and/or identification of water pollutants through simple and cost-effective approaches. For instance, methodologies involving fluorescence, absorbance, and electrochemistry, among others, have already being developed, demonstrating expansive research efforts toward water contaminants sensing.

Schematic illustration of a timeline displaying key events involving humankind and water. A brief description of each event can be seen. Clearly, an ambiguous relationship is noted since the number of contaminants has been increasing over time, but, at the same time, great efforts toward regulation/protection of water sources (e.g., drinking water) have been made.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water is an essential component of life on Earth. This “simple” substance composed of hydrogen and oxygen is mandatory for the survival of human beings. Despite this, the availability of drinking water as well as water sources has been a great concern since the beginning of the 19th century. The number of water contaminants has increased over time, causing enough pollution to restrict the use of the water sources worldwide. Some examples include heavy metals and nanoparticles as inorganic contaminants, and pharmaceuticals and explosives as organic contaminants. Taking this into account, there is an increasing demand for different nanosystems to perform the detection, quantification, and/or identification of water pollutants through simple and cost-effective approaches. For instance, methodologies involving fluorescence, absorbance, and electrochemistry, among others, have already being developed, demonstrating expansive research efforts toward water contaminants sensing.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water: The Key to Life on Earth
ce:section.ce:para: There is no doubt that water—a simple molecule composed of two atoms of hydrogen (H) and one of oxygen (O), which gives the general formula H2O—is the most important material on Earth. Therefore it may be surprising to know that water has a biography (Ball, 2001). Why is this molecule of such a simple chemical composition so important to life on Earth? The majority of living cells are compounded by a large amount (about half of the volume) of water in the liquid state. This liquid acts as a universal solvent and allows many physiological reactions to happen. Thus all lives on Earth are dependent on water (Henry, 2005). Furthermore, water’s properties also support life. For instance, a thin layer of ice (solid water) formed on the top of a lake protects all forms of lives in the lake by avoiding the freezing process of the entire water content in places where the temperature of the atmosphere falls below 0°C because the water under the thin ice layer remains at 4°C. This phenomenon of ice formation on the top of a lake is explained by the unique behavior of water’s density. In general, the density of a material increases with a decrease in the temperature. However, water’s density has a maximum value of around 4°C and starts to decrease at temperatures below or above this value. Thus the ice is less dense than the liquid water in the lake, which leads to the formation of a thin solid layer on the top of the water content. This layer “protects” the liquid water from extremely cold weather by halting the freezing process. The physical details about this process are not going to be elaborated on here as it is not the aim of this chapter. Readers can research the topic in the literature or via the Internet.

Ice forming on a lake is a clear example of how the properties of water directly protect lives on Earth; however, indirect situations can also be found. For example, the degree of solubility of gases like oxygen (O2) and carbon dioxide (CO2) in water are special requirements for sea life. This is highly significant for humans, since our species also depends on food from the sea. Humans have an intrinsic connection to water as determined by Mitchell et al. (1945). In their research, the authors investigated the chemical composition of an adult human body (35 years of age, 70.55kg in weight, and 183cm tall). Their findings revealed that approximately 70% of the studied body was made up of water. The water content of organs was also described. The heart, one of the most vital organs, contains 73.69% water, while the lungs contain 83.74%, the largest content. Even bones were found to be composed of 31.81% water. Fig. 3.1 is an illustration of the human body and the water percentages of some organs. Clearly, the high water percentage found in the human body is evidenced. After the work published by Mitchell et al. (1945) showed the large amount of water in the human body, other studies were performed. For example, Fomon et al. (1982) investigated the body composition of children from birth to 10 years of age, and found a decrease in the water content during the first 10 years of a person’s life. Schoeller (1989) further demonstrated the decrease of water content over age, supporting the results obtained by Fomon et al. (1982). Water plays a major role in the human body, given the large number of its functions, including as a solvent, reactant, product, catalyst, chaperone, messenger, and controller (Brini et al., 2017). Therefore drinking water is an essential nutrient for the survival of life on Earth.
aThe value of percentage is related to the water content found in the brain, spinal cord, and nerve trunks.

According to the World Health Organization (WHO, 2011), a person should drink an amount of water equal to their body losses and enough to avoid adverse effects, such as dehydration. Specifically, for a 70kg human body in a temperate zone, the minimum amount of drinking water is 3L per day. This amount changes according to age and gender, but it is important to highlight that the human body needs water every day in order to maintain the water balance and stay healthy. Moreover, clean water is necessary for personal sanitation. The WHO reports that each person requires 50–100L of water per day in order to accomplish their most basic needs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing Water Contaminants: A Brief Overview
ce:section.ce:para: After the previous section, it seems like nonsense to have a topic about the sensing of contaminants since the importance of water for the survival of the species living on Earth is demonstrated. However, water has surprisingly been a concern since the beginning of the 19th century. Still in 1900, Spiegel published a research paper about the significance of the contamination of drinking water by nitrite species (Spiegel, 1900). Moreover, the purification of drinking water by ozone was exposed in the World’s Fair in Paris (Thomae, 1900). Two years later (1902), Savage showed the importance of checking for the presence of a bacteria named Bacillus coli in water (Savage, 1902). In 1904, Vaughan was capable of detecting some toxicogenic germs in drinking water (Vaughan, 1904) by developing a new methodology (Michigan method) to perform bacteriological examination. Later, the industrial revolution as well as both World Wars increased the amount and diversity of contaminants in water, since huge growth in the manufacturing and use of synthetic chemicals was observed (Fu et al., 2013). For instance, waste disposal practices related to the military production during World War II have significantly contaminated the groundwater with common explosives like TNT (2,4,6-trinitrotoluene), RDX (hexahydro-1,3,5-trinitro-1,3,5-triazine), and HMX (octahydro-1,3,5,7-tetranitro-1,3,5,7-tetrazocine) (Pennington and Brannon, 2002). The presence of organohalides in chlorinated drinking water was reported in 1974 (Bellar et al., 1974).

Over the past few decades, the term “emergent contaminants” has appeared to denote chemicals associated to frequently used consumer, health-care, and personal-care products (Glassmeyer et al., 2017). Although, this may guide readers to assume that these contaminants have recently been released into the environment, which is a misunderstanding. In the 1970s and 1980s, the occurrence of domestic use–related compounds in wastewater (Shuval and Gruener, 1973), surface water (Donaldson, 1977; Richardson and Bowron, 1985), and drinking water (Coleman et al., 1980) were already reported. In this sense, the literature (Glassmeyer et al., 2017) cites “contaminants of emerging concern” as a more appropriate term to denote, for example, pharmaceutical substances like antibiotics (Karthikeyan and Meyer, 2006), hormones (Cartinella et al., 2006), and others (Kolpin et al., 2002). Given the contaminants mentioned here, it may be assumed that anything in water is a contaminant. Thus it is worth mentioning the definition of water contaminants. In this sense, the Safe Drinking Water Act (SDWA) defines a contaminant as any physical, chemical, biological, or radiological substance or matter in water. However, three criteria must be met for a substance to be considered a contaminant according to the United States Environmental Protection Agency (US EPA). First, the substance must have an adverse effect on human health. The other two are related to the occurrence of contaminants in drinking water: the levels of public health concern and/or the sole judgment of US EPA administrators. Based on these requirements, the first Drinking Water Contaminant Candidate List (CCL 1) was announced by the US EPA in 1998. The CCL is a list of contaminants that are not under any national regulation, but may be present in public water systems. Therefore the listed contaminants are expected to have future regulations. The CCL 1 was found to contain microbial and chemical contaminants as candidates. For instance, sulfate, sodium, perchlorate, and nitrobenzene are some chemical contaminants considered in the CCL 1. Subsequently, in 2003, the US EPA announced a formal decision to develop regulations. In 2016, the final CCL 4 was published by the US EPA. The list contains a total of 97 chemicals and 12 microbial contaminants, which includes various substances from those used in commerce to biological toxins and others. A chemical contaminant considered in the CCL 4 was ethylene glycol, a chemical largely used as antifreeze, in textile manufacturing, and that used to be used as pesticide. Other examples are acrolein, benzyl chloride, methanol, and nitroglycerin, among others. Fig. 3.2 presents a timeline summarizing some key events.

As it can be seen, the number of water contaminants has grown largely over the years (1900 until now) and each new one is going to need a regulation. However, this also implies a demand to develop a sensor system in order to monitor these. Thus technology to sense water contaminants has greatly advanced in the past few years. Many sensor systems have been developed to detect different analytes, like those previously mentioned and others of emerging concern (Glassmeyer et al., 2017), since researchers worldwide are developing distinct nanomaterial-based devices (Mao et al., 2015). Taking these into consideration, the next sections deal with traditional and modern methodologies, as well as nanomaterials frequently applied to detect water contaminants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: How to Sense Contaminants: Examples of Traditional and Modern Techniques
ce:section.ce:para: The ground water represents the most important source of potable water in the world, approximately 97%. Despite to be less accessible to pollution because is found underground, it has become increasingly vulnerable daily, especially from industrial and household waste, attracting more attention of researchers. Since water quality is an essential condition to the human development and well-being, as said by WHO, to evaluate the safety of the available water is increasingly relevant to protect the public health and the environment. There are many conventional ways to verify its safety, but mostly them are laborious, uncertain and expensive. (Lapworth et al., 2012; Palamuleni and Akoth, 2015). In this context, many techniques have raised to detect contamination traces and to help in the reduction of this critical issue. Hazardous substances are constantly released, as pesticides, herbicides, nitrate, antibiotic, organic compounds, and heavy metals that even at lower concentrations show extremely toxic to the ecosystem. If combined the presence of them with the long-term of exposure, the effects observed can be worse. Although the accumulation of organic materials to be also a serious matter, the heavy metals ions have been focus about to concern in the find of innovative ways to detect those target molecules that cause contamination. Heavy metal ions are nonbiodegradable and lead to risks to human health and environment. Arsenic, cadmium, mercury, and chromium are some striking examples of extremely toxic heavy metals that accumulate in the biosphere and living organisms, entering through the alimentary chain (Li et al., 2013a). Their mechanism of action can involve enzyme inhibition, oxidative stress, and impaired oxidant metabolism (Gumpu et al., 2015).

International and national agencies determine acceptable quality levels of some compounds in the water, establishing certain standard parameters. About microbiological analysis, fecal and total coliforms work as indicators of the presence of pathogenic organisms. In the physicochemical analysis, temperature, pH, turbidity, color, toughness and concentration of nitrate, chlorine and aluminum have an already delimited allowable value range. Such procedure mainly uses colorimetric methods, which is based on change in the color to identify the substance. Unfortunately, both techniques are time-consuming and can provide unreliable results due to the interfering agents' presence. Another way to measure the quality of waste and natural water is to determine the content of organic material. In this sense, methods such as chemical oxygen demand (COD), biochemical oxygen demand (BOD), and total organic carbon (TOC) are traditionally applied. COD involves a colorimetric method, which indirectly shows the amount of organic matter by evaluating the quantity of oxygen that would be consumed through chemical oxidation of several organic compounds in the liquid, without the intervention of microorganisms. Compared to BOD is very simple and fast, being used when the results obtained by this method is imprecise due to the toxic compounds released by microorganisms. In the end, the COD obtained can be expressed in mg O2 L−1. Meanwhile, BOD uses an electrometric method and consists of the amount of oxygen dissolved in the aquatic environment that is consumed during the biochemical degradation of organic matter by aerobic organisms. According to the regulations of the CETESB Sao Paulo State Environment Agency (Companhia de Tecnologia de Saneamento Ambiental), this kind of procedure is still limited, because of the presence of various interfering factors like temperature, pH, incubation time, and luminosity. In addition, the TOC content allows for the identification of contaminated water by organic impurities, by oxidizing them and detecting the carbon dioxide content (Carstea et al., 2016). Both COD and BOD are traditionally applied to monitor the environment. However, these techniques are laborious, prone to errors, and hazardous reagents are needed in the experimental procedure. Thus easy, fast and cheap techniques to monitor the environment are highly desired.

Many researchers worldwide have affirmed the potential of fluorescence spectroscopy for wastewater quality assessment of raw sewage and effluents, as it provides rapid feedback and requires little sample preparation. Such technique analyzes the fluorescence proprieties from a sample. Briefly, the fluorescence phenomenon can be defined as a release of energy in the form of light that occurs when there is the excitation of fluorophores, which are normally based in organic reporters. Other examples of useful advanced analytic techniques with potential applicability to monitor the environment include atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) and inductively coupled plasma-mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) (Kuang et al., 2013). However the sample preparation for these techniques may be associated with liquid chromatography (LC), gas chromatography (GC), or capillary electrophoresis (CE) (Zhang et al., 2016). This may make the process onerous and increase the time of analysis. Also, specialized personnel can be required to manipulate these equipments, which further increases the cost (Gumpu et al., 2015). Given this, there is still a great search for rapid, sensitive, specific, and cheap methods that allow to monitore the quality of water in natural and engineered systems by in situ analyses. In this sense, it is sought after to integrate what already exists and create platforms to reach enhanced ways of detecting contaminants. Since nanostructures provide more sensitivity, selectivity and resistance, alternative probes nano-based have been produced to sense contaminants in environmental samples (Gumpu et al., 2015). Therefore the combination of modern techniques and nanomaterials can overcome some limitations. Moreover, the developed sensing platforms can be miniaturized in a device of easy manipulation for nonspecialized personal use, contributing for portability and robustness. Like this versatility and selectivity are great advantages of these improved platforms, which may contain a molecule to recognize the target analyte. Classical examples of these compounds are nucleic acids (aptamers), antibodies, and enzymes (Orellana and Haigh, 2008). The signal emitted is proportional to the analyte concentration. According to purpose, other features may also be important, such as spectrum of performance, limiting concentrations and stability. A broad spectrum of performance and a low limiting concentration indicate the capability to detect and differentiate a variety of chemical compounds and pollutants. (Turner and Wilson, 1989). Besides the stability in terms of operational and storage are dependent on the immobilization process, as well as the possibility of reuse. This important attribute would help to reduce the cost of the production and analysis. Designed for the purpose, sensing platforms can be simpler than expected, for example, a fiber optic–based dip-in probe for remote measurements of pH, CO2, and O2 in environmental samples. In this case, the probe can be susceptible to chemical attack by harmful substances present in the liquid (Grieshaber et al., 2008).

In addition, the use of distinct nanomaterials has resulted in better amplification of the signal, faster response times, higher electron transfer rates and lower detection limit. Taking these improvements into consideration, the most used nanomaterials are carbon nanotubes, graphene, quantum dots, and metallic nanoparticles (NPs), especially gold NPs (AuNPs) (Martín-Yerga et al., 2012). Each one of these nanostructures has shown some kind of special property, which is used to build sensing protocol. For instance, metallic NPs display electronic particularities due to free electrons in the valence band. As a consequence, excitation by a light source can generate an oscillation of the electromagnetic wave on the surface. When the oscillation frequency is equal to the incident electromagnetic radiation, a unique effect called plasmon resonance can be observed. Because of this quantum effect, these nanostructures can be used to enhance screen-printing electrodes (SPE), since they act as electrochemical transducers improving the quality of the SPE technology (Martín-Yerga et al., 2012; Siangproh et al., 2011). Fig. 3.3 depicts the process of SPE fabrication. Furthermore, the screen-printing methodology has allowed a wider diversity of applications regarding the construction of electrochemical sensors, because it provides cost-effectiveness and practicality.

A sensing device that has a large importance to biotechnological applications is the biosensor. This analytical device contains a biological recognition component and a signal transducer combined with electronic systems, permitting to transform biological information into measurable and processable signals. Its type is determinated in accordance with the mechanisms of energy conversion used by the transducer. In this regard, it is summarized on electrochemical, piezoelectric, thermometric and optical (Verma and Gupta, 2015). Among these mechanisms, electrochemical methods have been highlighted due to the possibility of designing miniaturized electronic devices and also analyze biological samples. The resize of these platforms allows savings during the process of both analyte and steps. In addition, the electrochemical conversion involved deals with mass-transfer rate, electrolyte transport and distribution, as found at aqueous samples. According to the electrochemical procedure involved, the detection method can be classified as follows:
•
Amperometric: when a charge transfer occurs after the establishment of a potential difference, allowing for the quantification of the species in the medium through equilibrium displacement. Among electro-analytical techniques, cyclic voltammetry is most commonly used.
•
Potentiometric: this is based on voltage differences and is considered to be nondestructive because the substrate is not consumed.
•
Conductometric: this is based on the change in electrode conductance provoked by ionic concentration.
•
Impedimetric: this is when the current impedance variation on the electrode is evaluated from biomolecule recognition at the surface (Bahadir and Sezgintürk, 2016).

At present, the most used are sensors based on amperometric and potentiometric methods (Han et al., 2001; Mulchandani et al., 2001; Siangproh et al., 2011; Turner and Wilson, 1989; Wang et al., 2011).

Urbanization, as well as the increasing world population, has been causing an increasing generation of waste due to human activities over the decades (Hoornweg et al., 2013). As a consequence, there is an urgent demand for clean and safe water. Considering this, it is essential that the sensing techniques of toxic chemicals take a step forward toward better detection protocols regarding selectivity, rapid acquisition, reproducibility, easy handling of apparatus, and the ability to get results from real-world samples. Taking this information into account, this section focuses on new trends and innovative approaches to sense inorganic and organic contaminants in water.

There is not a clear classification for elements that are put under the term “heavy metals” (Duffus, 2002). Usually cadmium, copper, mercury, arsenic, and lead are denoted by this term. Even though some are considered essential trace elements for biochemical reactions in plants and humans (e.g., copper), others may be hazardous and toxic even in small amounts (e.g., arsenic, lead). Heavy metals are naturally occurring elements, which could be present in water due to natural processes like volcanic eruptions and the weathering of rocks and soil, for example. However, the leaking of industrialization by-products into water sources and other anthropogenic actions (agriculture and domestic activities) are commonly pointed out as being responsible for high levels of heavy metals in water. This leads to environmental pollution, which affects living organisms (Tchounwou et al., 2012). The ability of heavy metals to cause serious diseases motivates researchers to develop assays to detect their presence in any given environment. Table 3.1 provides recommended standards for heavy metals in drinking water (Aragay et al., 2011).

Classic techniques employed for heavy metal ion detection in samples include ICP-MS, mass spectroscopy, X-ray fluorescence spectroscopy, and AAS. However, such techniques require sophisticated material, trained personnel, and adequate infrastructure (Aragay et al., 2011). Therefore studies are aiming at detecting small quantities of these kinds of metals using adaptable and simple techniques based on colorimetric, fluorimetric, and electrochemical approaches (Aragay et al., 2011; Gumpu et al., 2015). Some of the strategies being developed to detect heavy metal ions mainly in aqueous environments are highlighted in the following sections.

When exposed to lead (Pb), the human body can undergo several deleterious effects due to oxidative stress. It may affect the reproductive, cardiovascular, renal, hematopoietic, and/or nervous system. Pb2+ is neither necessary nor beneficial to the body. Moreover, this metal is nonbiodegradable, and happens to persist for long periods in the environment (Flora et al., 2012). In this regard, the development of sensors for the detection of Pb is important.

Among many detection strategies for metal cations, the formation of G-quadruplex has been extensively studied (Bhattacharyya et al., 2016). A G-quadruplex consists of at least two layers of G-quartets, which are formed by four guanines. The G-quadruplex is stabilized by the presence of a central cation, which is coordinated by the hydrogen-bonded DNA base quartets. Although not all cations are adequate for quadruplex stabilization (Bourdoncle et al., 2006), Pb2+ is vastly used in this regard (Li et al., 2017a; Zhan et al., 2014). Using this approach, Li et al. (2013c) developed a sensor capable of detecting as low as 90pM of Pb2+. In their system, aptamer-functionalized CdSe/ZnS quantum dots (QDs) were assembled with graphene oxide (GO), which quenches fluorescence due to energy transfer. However, the interaction between Pb2+ ions and the aptamer (a single-stranded guanine-rich DNA sequence) is strong enough to displace the GO and form a G-quadruplex/Pb2+. Now, the free aptamer-functionalized QDs emit fluorescence (“turn-on”). A schematic illustration explaining the sensing mechanism is displayed in Fig. 3.4 .

Similarly, a layer-by-layer technique was employed in order to produce CdSe/CdS QDs functionalized with xylenol orange (XO) for the detection of Pb2+. The XO was self-assembled in fluorescent chitosan-capped QDs, forming stable conjugates with quenched photoluminescence (PL) due to electron transfer from XO and the QDs. The PL was recovered upon the addition of Pb2+ due to its high affinity to XO, disrupting the previous electron transfer mechanism (Fig. 3.5 ). The sensor achieved a detection limit of 0.02µM (Zhao et al., 2013). Besides the applicability of QDs, it is important to note that toxic elements like cadmium and selenium are present in their composition. Therefore new environment-friendly sensors are being developed. A highly selective and biocompatible nanosensor for Pb2+ detection was developed based on photoinduced electron transfer (PET) between aptamer-modified reduced graphene QDs (rGQDs) and GO. The assembly of aptamer-rGQDs and GO leads to near total fluorescence quenching of the previously fluorescent GQDs. The addition of Pb2+ ions causes the formation of a complex with the rGQD structure, thus causing a separation of GO and a turning on of the fluorescence from the rGQDs again, which increases with the addition of Pb2+. The biosensor showed a detection limit of 0.6nM and good selectivity when compared to other cations (Qian et al., 2015).

AuNPs are also frequently utilized in sensor production for Pb detection (Lu et al., 2015; Taghdisi et al., 2016). For instance, Chu et al. coupled AuNPs with glutathione (GSH) in order to detect Pb2+ ions. The researchers hypothesized that GSH would interact with Pb2+ ions and a core satellite would be formed by self-assembly. As a consequence, a solution color change from red to blue was observed, as well as the solution being selective toward Pb2+ when compared to other divalent cations (Chu et al., 2015). In addition, mesoporous silica (MS) NPs have attracted significant attention in electrochemical studies due to their large surface area and narrow pore size, among other features. Consequently, they are regarded as a good inorganic support system for nanocomposite production. Hollow silica microspheres with an ultrathin wall, functionalized with amine groups (NH2-HSM), were utilized for electrochemical sensing of trace Pb2+ by square wave anodic stripping voltammetry. The ultrathin wall, which aids in a faster electron transfer, coupled with the hollow morphology and the high surface area of the NH2-HSM/glassy carbon electrode showed high sensitivity and reusability. Finally, it showed a detection limit of 0.5nM and was applicable for river water sample analysis (Hao et al., 2016).

Although copper (Cu) is an essential element to the health and efficient functioning of several living organisms, inadequate intake (deficiency or excess) by the human body can contribute to the development of many diseases and abnormalities. Copper toxicity can lead to, among other symptoms, liver disease, anemia, neurological defects, and acute toxicity which could result in death in the most severe cases (Uriu-Adams and Keen, 2005). Since accidental ingestion of copper could happen through drinking water, accounting for an unknown amount of daily intake, the detection of copper ions should be further studied in order to develop more simple, selective, biocompatible, and fast sensors.

Functionalized MS was utilized in copper determination in a carbon paste electrode. Its mechanical stability was coupled with a naturally occurring copper ion–specific tripeptide, Gly-Gly-His. This tripeptide can form a stabile chelate combination with Cu2+, coordinating with the ion through four of its five nitrogen atoms. Linear seep anodic stripping voltammetry showed a better sensitivity of the Gly-Gly-His modified electrode toward the presence of Cu2+ when compared to the electrode containing only MS. This result is due to the specific binding of the tripeptide. Moreover, the sensor showed applicability when testing tap water samples (Mureşeanu et al., 2014).

Human exposure to mercury (Hg) results mainly from dental amalgam and through ingestion of mercury present in seafood, with considerable amounts of mercury present in fish that are at the top of the food chain. Poisoning with mercuric salts can lead to vomiting, diarrhea, renal tubular necrosis, asthma, and the suppression of natural killer cells, among other symptoms. Other forms of mercury, like inorganic mercury (Hg0, Hg2 ++, and Hg++) and organic mercury (compounds in which mercury is bonded to methyl, ethyl, and other similar groups), compounds can damage other body tissues (Bernhoft, 2012). Therefore developing sensors for mercury monitoring is of extreme importance.

A pyrazole-functionalized Fe3O4@SiO2 magnetic nanocomposite was developed for the detection of Hg2+. The nanocomposite showed significant quenching of its previous fluorescence when Hg2+ was added to the aqueous solution, which was explained by the simultaneous binding of the ion to the pyrazole-based ligand. The study showed that with increasing Hg2+ concentration, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased, and the detection did not suffer interference by other cations. It showed a detection limit of 7.6nM and the advantage of the easy separation of the nanocomposite from the aqueous solution due to its magnetic nature (Mir et al., 2017).

DNA-based sensors have been developed based on the selective binding of Hg2+ to thymine bases (Wang et al., 2016b; Zhang et al., 2015). In a study, a DNA-based sensor that utilized GO as a fluorophore was developed by researchers. The single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) aptamer molecules are deposited on the surface of GO sheets. Thus in the presence of Hg2+, a hairpin-shaped double-stranded DNA structure was formed due to the coordination of the thymine base with Hg2+ (T-Hg2+-T), and due to the proximity of the GO and the Hg2+ ions, electrons were transferred from GO to Hg2+, leading to the quenching of the GO fluorescence. A schematic illustration of this mechanism can be seen in Fig. 3.6 . The high specificity of the base toward Hg2+ led to a high selectivity when compared to other metal ions and a detection limit of 0.92nM (Li et al., 2013b).

AuNPs were employed in another approach used to sense Hg2+ ions based on the formation of a T-Hg-T complex. T-rich single-stranded oligonucleotides were bound to AuNPs by a thiol group at the 5′-end (forming Au−S covalent bonds) and a fluorophore at the 3′-end. In the presence of Hg2+ ions, a hairpin-like structure is formed and the fluorophore (FAM) is brought closer to the AuNP, quenching its fluorescence due to energy transfer from the dye to the nanoparticle (Tan et al., 2013). A similar approach utilized the same principle of ssDNA-attached AuNPs, where the oligonucleotide formed a hairpin-like structure when Hg2+ was added to the solution, but instead of analyzing the resultant fluorescence difference, the changes were able to be observed by the naked eye. The formation of the hairpin on the surface of negative AuNPs decreases the electrostatic repulsion between adjacent particles. This causes aggregation and, therefore, a color change is easily detectable. The system showed a detection limit of 50nM Hg2+ for pond and river water (Chen et al., 2014).

Inorganic arsenic is usually found in the environment as arsenite (As(III)) and/or arsenate (As(V)) salts. Arsenic is a highly toxic element, which could lead to diseases in multiple organs. Moreover, long-lasting exposure is associated with various malignancies (Ratnaike, 2003). Therefore approaches to perform arsenic detection are highly desired. In this sense, a glass carbon electrode modified with platinum NPs and carbon nanotubes (PtNP/CNT/GCE) was used to detect As(III) (Xu et al., 2008a). The PtNP/CNT/GCE electrode displayed a better performance regarding electrochemical response, in the presence of As(III), when compared to PtNP/GCE alone. The enhanced response was attributed to the larger effective surface area due to the reduced size of the NPs supported on the surface of the CNTs, which led to large local rates of mass transport. The composite was found to have a low detection limit of 0.12ppb. Although, there is still a need to achieve practicability for the measurement of real samples and to assess interferences from other metals.

AuNPs were also being employed in the detection of arsenic through various approaches (Domínguez-González et al., 2014; Ottakam Thotiyl et al., 2012). For instance, surface-modified AuNPs were utilized to detect arsenic through a dynamic light scattering technique. To accomplish this, GSH, dithiothreitol (DTT), and cysteine (Cys) were first conjugated to the surface of AuNPs. In the presence of As(III) ions, the surface-modified NPs were observed to aggregate due to the binding of the ions. This led to a colorimetric change in the sample, as well as a variation in the intensity of the dynamic light scattering. The assay showed a high sensitivity toward As(III) ions, and the ability to detect as little as 3ppt arsenic in aqueous solutions, as well as selectivity when compared to other heavy metal ions. The assay also demonstrated good reproducibility and potential practical applications (Kalluri et al., 2009).

Most assays focus on the detection of arsenite instead of arsenate, but the literature does contain a few examples of arsenate sensors (Chaniotakis et al., 2005; Das and Sarkar, 2016). Lopez et al. developed a biosensor for As(V) based on fluorescent-labeled DNA adsorbed on the surface of CeO2 and CePO4 NPs. The fluorescent DNA showed fluorescence quenching due to its adsorption on the NPs via its phosphate backbone. However, the presence of arsenate on the surface of the NPs led to a displacement of the DNA from the NP surface, thus recovering its fluorescence. In comparison to other anions, the DNA-capped CeO2 NPs showed a high selectivity toward As(V) when compared to As(III), with a detection limit of 29nM (Lopez et al., 2017).

Since water sources might be contaminated with multiple pollutants, sensors able to perform selective and/or multiple detection of heavy metals and toxic chemicals from real-world samples are highly desirable. However, background contaminants and interference in detection are still major drawbacks. Taking this into consideration, Ding et al. were able to develop a bifunctional fluorescent sensor based on the surface functionalization of silica microspheres with a 2,2-dipicolyamine derivative (Ding et al., 2015). The system was able to detect and remove Cd2+, Hg2+, and Pb2+, achieving detection limits of 36, 48, and 39nM, respectively. Furthermore, the developed sensor was also applicable for tap water and wastewater, which evidenced its practical use for real-world samples. In an electrochemical approach, Xu et al. developed an electrode made of multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) dispersed in Nafion combined with a bismuth film in order to determine trace Pb(II) and Cd(II). Due to the synergistic effect of the MWCNTs and the bismuth film, along with its simple operation and inexpensive instrumentation, the method showed good sensitivity (25ngL−1 for Pb and 40ngL−1 for Cd) as well as reproducibility (Xu et al., 2008b).

Paper-based sensors are one of the most promising systems for the development of cheap and simple sensing devices for heavy metal detection (Hossain and Brennan, 2011; Lin et al., 2016). This kind of system was utilized for the electrochemical (Pb2+ and Cd2+) and colorimetric (Cu2+) detection of metal ions in the same study (Chaiyo et al., 2016). For the colorimetric detection of Cu2+, cetyltrimethylammonium bromide (CTAB)-modified silver nanoplates (AgNPls) containing thiosulfate (
S
2
O
3
2 −
) were synthesized, and with the addition of Cu(II), the AgNPls underwent a decline in size due to the catalytic etching of the particles by the formation of Cu
(
S
2
O
3
)
3
5 −
. This results in a color change from pinkish violet to colorless. Bismuth-coated electrodes were manufactured in the same paper sensor for trace detection of Pb(II) and Cd(II) using anodic stripping voltammetry, which showed well-defined stripping peaks for Pb(II) and Cd(II).

Fluoride (F−) is a biologically important ion due to its beneficial action, mainly in orthodontics and osteoporosis treatments. Fluoride is usually added in low concentrations to drinking water in order to prevent undesirable symptoms in the population, like dental caries and bone fracture. However, this is a controversial topic, since excess fluoride can cause adverse health effects, like skeletal and dental fluorosis (Everett, 2011; Sharma et al., 2017). Furthermore, it is important to note that the maximum level of fluoride allowed in potable water was set to between 1.4 and 2.4ppm by the US EPA. Although, the amount of fluoride in the human body could be higher due to fluoride exposure through drinking water, food, dental products, and pesticides (major sources). Taking this into account, this section focuses on different approaches for the detection of fluoride in aqueous samples.

A simple method for fluoride detection, based on NP aggregation, was developed by Boken et al. AuNPs modified with 2-thiobarbituric acid were synthesized in order to detect the presence of fluoride. The presence of fluoride anions induced the aggregation of the NPs. The observed aggregation did not occur in the presence of other halide anions. It was possible to visually detect a change in color of the AuNP suspension when fluoride was present, with a visual detection limit of 10mM in a pH range of 7–8 (Boken et al., 2015). In another colorimetric approach, fluoride capping was found to increase the oxidase activity of nanoceria (CeO2 NPs), which enabled the detection of fluoride anions in the presence of 2,2′-azinobis(3-ethylbenzothiozoline)-6-sulfonic acid (ABTS). ABTS is colorless in water and mixing fluoride ions with ABTS shows no color change. However, in the presence of fluoride-capped nanoceria, the color changes to green, with a higher color intensity as the fluoride concentration increases. The method also showed a high selectivity for fluoride ions when compared to other anions, with a detection limit of 0.64µM in water and toothpaste (Liu et al., 2016).

A colorimetric sensor was developed based on the strong binding between silicon and fluoride, and this was applied to silicon nanowires (SiNWs). In the presence of a CTAB solution, the previously aggregated SiNW with H-terminated surfaces untied and formed a dispersion in water with a yellow color. When fluoride ions were introduced to the system, it replaced the aliphatic CTAB chain because of the strong F–Si bond. Thus the fluoride anions attract the ammonium cations, exposing the CTAB aliphatic chain to water, which induces the formation of clusters. The formation of clusters changes the color of the system from yellow to transparent. This is a simple, label-free, selective, and stable colorimetric sensor system for the detection of fluoride in pure water phase, with a detection limit of 1ppm (Wang et al., 2015).

Silica-based fluoride sensors are a promising inorganic support, with MS being targeted for functionalization with fluoride sensing molecules due to its defined pores, as well as the possibility of recycling the nanomaterial for repeated detection cycles. A naphthalene urea fluorescent receptor was immobilized in MS in order to detect fluoride ions. Upon the addition of fluoride ions to a suspension of the fluorescent receptor immobilized mesoporous silica (FMS) in water, the fluorescence of the receptor was quenched, due to a reverse PET when the ions behave as PET donors. Fluoride causes the strongest fluorescence quenching when compared to Cl−, Br−, I−, and
HSO
4
−
. In addition, increasing additions of fluoride anions caused the fluorescence to gradually decrease, indicating the selectivity and quantitative binding of fluoride to the urea moiety of the FMS. In addition, the decreased fluorescence following contact with fluoride was found to be reversible after rinsing with distilled water, thereby showing its reusability (Seo et al., 2008). MS was also functionalized with a receptor which possessed anthraquinone as a fluorescent unit and urea as a fluoride ion–binding site (AFMS); a decline in fluorescence was observed when fluoride ions were added to a suspension of AFMS in water. Fluorescence quenching did not occur upon the addition of Cl−, Br−, I−, and
HSO
4
−
as well. The sensor showed a detection limit of 0.50µM and the fluorescence was found to be recoverable after rinsing with distilled water, showing excellent reusability and almost no loss of sensitivity even after eight repeated cycles (Kim et al., 2008).

A PL on–off–on probe based on CdTe QDs was synthesized for the detection of fluoride ions. The CdTe QDs were capped with 3-mercaptopropanoic acid (MPA), which formed a fluorescent water-soluble dispersion. However, when Eu(NO3)3 is added to the system, the Eu3+ ions coordinate to the free carboxylate groups of MPA that are on the surface of the QDs, resulting in its aggregation and further PL quenching through electron transfer processes. However, when fluoride ions are added to the system, the aggregates get dissociated. This dissociation happens due to the higher affinity of fluoride to Eu3+ when compared to the affinity of Eu3+ to carboxylate groups, thus restoring the PL of the QDs (Fig. 3.7 ). The nanocomposite showed selectivity toward fluoride ions when compared to substances such as thiosulfate, nitrate, sulfate, cations (e.g., Fe2+, Ni2+) and amino acids (proline, serine, alanine, and histidine). It showed a detection limit of 5ppm and complete detection in less than 10seconds (Singh et al., 2017). Although the use of quantum dots leads to great sensitivity, it is important to look for less toxic compounds. Given this, more attention should be given to environment-friendly approaches. Singhal et al. developed a PL on–off–on carbon quantum dot (CQD) sensor for fluoride ions, following the same principle of the high affinity of F− to Eu3+ (Singhal et al., 2017). Due to the biocompatibility, high aqueous stability, and low toxicity of CQDs, it makes them promising candidates for future QD-based detection systems.

Engineered nanomaterials, although beneficial in several fields of study, may lead to unknown consequences in the environment (Garner and Keller, 2014; Wiesner et al., 2006). Nanomaterials are considered as pollutants of emerging concern (US EPA, 2017). Emerging contaminants are synthetic or naturally occurring chemicals, many of which are nonregulated and not commonly monitored, with the potential of causing detrimental effects to the environment (Geissen et al., 2015). Due to the different characteristics of nanomaterials (e.g., composition, morphology, surface charge) it is still a challenge to develop universal management methodologies. According to the US EPA, nanomaterials in drinking water may be subject to the SDWA. However, there are no maximum contaminant levels (MCLs) or maximum contaminant level goals (MCKGs) established for nanomaterials yet (US EPA, 2017).

The detection of nanomaterials in environmental samples is a challenging objective. This field of study is still in its initial phase due to several drawbacks related to the matrix complexity and nature of nanomaterials. Although there is increasing understanding of the fate of nanomaterials in the environment (Baalousha et al., 2016; Dale et al., 2015; Klaine et al., 2008; Peijnenburg et al., 2015), the development of protocols for in situ monitoring is an ongoing mission. So far many conventional methods for the characterization of nanomaterials (e.g., single-particle ICP-MS, electron microscopy, light scattering) are being employed (commonly in tandem) for their detection in environmental water samples (Laborda et al., 2016; Leopold et al., 2016; Meermann and Laborda, 2015). But due to limitations intrinsic to each methodology, these methods are not suitable for in situ monitoring. Consequently, it is of utmost importance that simpler methodologies and sensors are developed for the detection of nanomaterials in environmental samples.

Electrode-particle impact is a promising approach when it comes to nanoparticle detection and characterization (Sokolov et al., 2017). It has been used to detect and study, among many others, gold (Zhou et al., 2012), platinum (Dasari et al., 2014), copper (Haddou et al., 2012), and nickel (Zhou et al., 2013) NPs, and even single-walled carbon nanotubes (Park et al., 2013) and core–shell particles (Holt et al., 2016). Briefly, an electrode is immersed in a solution which contains the analyte (in this case, NPs). The Brownian motion of the NPs causes their collision to the electrode surface, where they could adsorb, react, or enable a catalytic reaction. The resulting changes in the electrode current caused by the collisions are then measured and the analyte can be further recognized and inspected (Sokolov et al., 2017). With this technique, Stuart et al. were able to detect AgNPs in seawater samples (Stuart et al., 2013). The researchers observed a Faradaic charge transfer linked to the oxidation of the NPs which collided with the electrode surface. This method made it possible to study features like the size and aggregation of AgNPs and their behavior in seawater, showing its potential for use in complex matrices. A different electrochemical approach was performed by Li et al. for the detection of zinc (ZnNW) and silver oxide nanowires (AgNW) in aqueous samples (Li et al., 2017b). In order to separate the nanomaterials from dissolved ions with the same elements in solution, polycarbonate membranes coated with gold films were fabricated. In addition, the membranes were also utilized as a working electrode for differential pulse voltammetry measurements. This filtration method was capable of capturing ZnNWs and AgNWs and allowed for their quantitative and qualitative detection in aqueous solutions. The researchers were also able to identify and quantify AgNWs which were spiked in tap water, showing the possible applicability of this method in real-world samples.

Colorimetry is also a promising approach for facile in situ monitoring. He et al. developed a rapid and simple assay to visually detect the presence of MnO2 nanosheets in solution and environmental water samples (He et al., 2016). The nanosheets cause the oxidation of 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to TMB diamine, and this redox reaction leads to color changes. According to the concentration of MnO2, different colors could be observed, making it a semiquantitative detection. The researchers also showed the practicality of the system in real water samples. Alternatively, Corredor et al. (2015) developed a colorimetric method to detect different metal and oxide NPs in various complex matrices. To do this, the researchers took into account the surface catalytic redox properties of NPs. When NPs are present in a methylene blue (MB) and sodium borohydride (BH4) solution, it causes the electron transfer from BH4 to MB. The nanoparticle catalytic electron transfer process promotes the reduction of MB (a blue dye), originating leucomethylene blue (LMB), which is colorless. The color change (from blue to colorless) can be assessed by a portable UV–Vis. This method was capable of detecting AgNPs and AuNPs, as well as CeO2, SiO2, and VO2 NPs. In addition, the method showed good sensitivity for the detection of tannic acid–coated AuNPs in complex matrices, with a detection limit of 0.157ppm when testing lake water.

Although some specific classes of contaminants have been highlighted in the previous sections, it is absolutely necessary to shed some light on other contaminants which have not been mentioned. The detection of contaminants, such as cyanide, nitrate, nitrite, and hydrogen sulfide, should also be taken into consideration due to several deleterious effects in humans if these elements are present in environmental waters in exceeding amounts (Fan, 2011; Rubright et al., 2017; Vale, 2016).

Gold nanomaterials, as previously presented, are used for the detection of several inorganic contaminants. Liu et al. (2010) reported a gold-nanocluster (AuNC) fluorescent sensor for the detection of cyanide in water. The nanoclusters (formed by several to tens of atoms), with sizes of less than 1nm, were stabilized with bovine serum albumin (BSA). The addition of cyanide into the system resulted in the release of Au atoms from the fluorescent BSA-stabilized AuNCs, thus forming an gold cyanide complex. The formation of this complex resulted in fluorescence quenching, which was observed by the researchers. A low detection limit of 200nM was achieved by the sensor, which also showed selectivity toward cyanide when compared to other anions such as
N
3
−
,
NO
2
−
,
Cl
−
,
SO
4
2 −
, among others. Moreover, the method was also applicable for detection in different water samples, such as groundwater, pond, tap, and lake water. AuNCs were also utilized by researchers for the detection of hydrogen sulfide, a flammable and highly toxic gas (Zhang et al., 2017). Acetylcysteine-stabilized AuNC (ACC@AuNC)-based fluorescent sensors were developed. Upon interaction of fluorescent ACC@AuNCs with hydrogen sulfide, the fluorescence of the system was quenched, an effect that can be monitored. The fluorescence quenching induced by the introduction of hydrogen sulfide was attributed to the formation of Au2S and the increased size of the AuNCs. The sensor showed a detection limit of 1.8nM and applicability for tap and river water samples. Alternatively, a nanocomposite made of AuNPs decorated with reduced graphene oxide (AuNP-rGO) was developed by Amanulla et al. (2017) for the detection of nitrite in water. Upon the addition of nitrite to aqueous solutions containing chitosan-stabilized AuNP-rGO, the previously red color of the composite turned purple. The color change was assessed by UV–Vis, but was also easily detectable by the naked eye. Furthermore, the detection limit achieved by the sensor was 0.1µm under optimized conditions, and it showed applicability for tap water monitoring.

Silica NPs have also been applied for the detection of such contaminants. As an example, Sayed et al. (2017) reported a fluorescent sensor for cyanide detection. The pores of MS were loaded with a dye, namely [Ru(bipy)3]2+. In addition, the external surface of the MS was functionalized with a nickel(II)-macrocyclic complex. Finally, the particles were capped with hexametaphosphate, a sterically hindering anion. The presence of cyanide ions induced the highly favored demetallation of nickel(II) complexes and the removal of the capping anions from the particle surface. This process leads to the release of the previously loaded dye, consequently increasing the fluorescence intensity of the system. Moreover, the sensor showed a detection limit of 2µm and was able to detect cyanide in tap water with good response.

Finally, it is worth mentioning that certain electrochemical approaches can be applied for the detection of some of these contaminants. The detection of nitrate in water was performed by copper electrodes obtained from copper nanowires. In this regard, a porous nanowire network was first produced by the thermal annealing of copper nanowires at 600°C. The electrode was used as a sensor for nitrate detection by nitrate reduction (in an acid medium) through differential pulse voltammetry. It showed a detection limit of 1.35µM as well as a wide range of detection, being suitable for water samples with extremely low or extremely high nitrate concentrations. According to the authors, the performance of the sensor may be due to the superior electrical conductivity of copper nanowires, high specific surface area coupled with better access of
NO
3
−
to active sites via capillarity. The sensor also showed applicability when testing different water samples (Liang et al., 2016).

A carbon paste electrode was modified with AuNPs/MWCNTs for the determination of nitrite, and showed good electrocatalytic activity and voltammetric performance regarding oxidation. It showed good selectivity toward nitrite since several ions (like Na+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Cl−, and F−) did not disturb the detection. The sensor displayed a detection limit of 0.01µM and it was successfully applied to identify nitrite in different samples, including mineral and tap water (Afkhami et al., 2014). In another study, AuNPs were coupled with GO modified with amino groups in order to form a sensing element for nitrate ions (Ren et al., 2015). The system was able to detect as low as 5nM of nitrate ions in river water samples. Initially, the amino group–modified GO causes the aggregation of negatively charged AuNPs. However, in the presence of nitrate ions, these interact with the positively charged GO amino groups, decreasing the aggregation of AuNPs according to the concentration of nitrate, which is then assessed.

Alternatively, bimetallic nanomaterials offer great advantages when compared to single metal materials due to the synergistic effect of both compounds. A nanocomposite formed by nitrogen-doped graphene (NG) functionalized with 1,3,6,8-pyrene tetra sulfonic acid sodium salt (PyTS) and decorated with Au-Pt bimetallic NPs was utilized for the detection of nitrite. The nanocomposite showed superior catalytic activity for the oxidation of nitrite, which was assessed according to the amperometric response. The detection limit of this system was 0.19µM and showed good applicability when utilized for sensing nitrite in tap water (Li et al., 2016).

The presence of pharmaceuticals and hormones in aquatic environments is a growing concern. These compounds represent a risk to the health of humans and wildlife, as they can lead to the emergence of antibiotic-resistant bacteria in the environment and the disruption of the natural metabolism of animals, for example. These compounds have been detected in a wide range of water sources, including sewage flows, surface water and groundwater. The concentration of these pharmaceutical compounds generally ranges from trace to ppb levels, which is significantly lower than concentrations capable to affect aquatic organisms and others. These chemicals can reach the environment through many pathways, for example, human excretion, direct waste disposal, and/or veterinary use. The effects and risks of long-term exposure to pharmaceuticals at low levels are still not well understood (Fatta-Kassinos et al., 2011; Heberer, 2002; Richardson and Kimura, 2016). Examples of pharmaceuticals with high occurrence in water are antiinflammatories and analgesics (paracetamol, acetylsalicylic acid, ibuprofen, and diclofenac); antidepressants (benzodiazepines); antiepileptics (carbamazepine); lipid-lowering drugs (fibrates); β-blockers (atenolol, propranolol, and metoprolol); antiulcer drugs and antihistamines (ranitidine and famotidine); antibiotics (tetracyclines, macrolides, β-lactams, penicillins, quinolones, sulfonamides, fluoroquinolones, chloramphenicol, and imidazole derivatives); and other substances (cocaine, barbiturates, methadone, amphetamines, opiates, heroin, and other narcotics) (Rivera-Utrilla et al., 2013).

The pollution produced by these compounds has been acknowledged across the world, leading to the establishment of a research field known as pharmaceuticals in the environment. Environmental agencies in the United States and Europe are also making efforts to monitor the presence of these compounds in the environment (Richardson and Kimura, 2016; Rivera-Utrilla et al., 2013). In this sense, there are a number of analytical methods already established for the detection/quantification of these compounds, including thin-layer chromatography (TLC), gas chromatography coupled with mass spectroscopy (GC-MS) or electron capture (GC-EC), high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), CE, diode array (DA), flame ionization (FI), and enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA). However, these methods are often time-consuming, require complicated and sophisticated instruments for analysis, as well as highly trained technical personnel, and complex sample pretreatment processes. Therefore the development of an easy, cheap, fast, and robust system to sense pollutants in water sources is highly desired. In this regard, the use of nanosensors can surpass these problems since they could potentially provide easier operational procedures combined with faster response times and the possibility of automatization and miniaturization.

Antibiotics have great importance in the treatment of human and animal diseases. However, these compounds tend to accumulate in surface waters mainly via human intake followed by subsequent excretion into municipal wastewater, hospitals, pharmaceutical wastes, and landfills. Also, a great portion of antibiotics used in animal food production are inefficiently adsorbed in the gut of animals, resulting in an excretion rate between 30% and 90%. The accumulation of these compounds in the environment may cause long-term effects on soil, water, microorganisms, plants, and animals, which ultimately affects human health via the food chain. For these reasons, the detection of antibiotics in the environment is extremely important (Lan et al., 2017; Lei et al., 2015).

Various types of nanomaterials have been used alongside biomolecules for the detection of antibiotics. Some examples of nanomaterials include carbon nanomaterials, metal NPs, magnetic NPs, up-conversion NPs (UCNPs), and quantum dots. These materials have been used alongside biomolecules like enzymes, aptamers, and antibodies as recognition elements. The signal-transducing mechanisms of these platforms usually rely on optical and electrochemical transduction (Lan et al., 2017).

Fluorimetric and colorimetric assays have been used for qualitative and semiquantitative preliminary detection, since they can be simple and fast; however, they often have low sensitivity (Lan et al., 2017). The use of fluorescent nanobiosensors usually relies on Förster (or fluorescence) resonance energy transfer (FRET) phenomena. FRET consists of an energy transition between two molecules: a fluorophore and a quencher. These transitions are distance-dependent and can be exploited for quantifying biomolecular recognition events (Lan et al., 2017). Among nanomaterials, GO is frequently used as a fluorescent probe, by employing FRET phenomena. For example, a GO hydrogel prepared by a mix of GO sheets, adenosine, and aptamers was employed for fluorescent detection of oxytetracycline (Fig. 3.8 ). Aptamers are short nucleic acid fragments capable of binding target molecules with a high affinity and specificity. Under optimal conditions, the biosensing platform was able to quantify oxytetracycline in a linear range of 25–1000μgL−1, showing excellent accuracy for real water samples (Tan et al., 2016). The detection was based on the disruption of fluorescence quenching provided by the interaction between the aptamer and an antibiotic. Other nanomaterials, such as UCNPs, AuNPs, and CdTe QDs, have also been employed in fluorescent detection of antibiotics. Oleic acid–modified UCNPs were utilized for the fluorimetric detection of kanamycin. The bounding between kanamycin and aptamer caused conformational changes on the aptamer structure, leading to a block in the energy transfer of the UCNP–aptamer–GO complex. The sensor achieved a linear range from 0.01 to 3nM, with a detection limit of 18pM for aqueous buffer solutions (Li et al., 2014a). For colorimetric antibiotic detection systems, AuNPs have been more consistently used. In these systems, detection is achieved by AuNP aggregation, which induces color changes (Lan et al., 2017). AuNPs were coated with aptamers for the detection of aminoglycosides. In the presence of antibiotic analytes, the aptamers bind to the aminoglycosides, and then the stabilization of the AuNPs promoted by the aptamers is disrupted, leading to aggregation and changes in colorimetric response. A linear range between 1 and 100nM was achieved (Derbyshire et al., 2012).

Nanomaterials have also been exploited for antibiotic detection without the utilization of biomolecules (aptamers, enzymes, or antibodies) as recognition elements. PVP-stabilized AgNPs have been used for the quantification of sulfadiazine through cloud point extraction and indirect spectrophotometry at 457nm. The NPs were able to detect the antibiotic in a concentration of 2.8μgL−1 as a detection limit, with a dynamic linear range from 10 to 800μgL−1. Real samples (milk, urine, and well water) were used for recovery tests in this method, providing recovery rates between 95% and 99%. The method was able to provide fast spectrophotometric measurement in different samples in a simple, inexpensive, efficient, and environment-friendly way, presenting satisfactory precision and accuracy (Kazemi et al., 2017). AgNPs capped with β-cyclodextrin were used for metal-enhanced fluorescence in the detection of fluoroquinolones (ciprofloxacin, enrofloxacin, and lomefloxacin). This fluorimetric detection method was able to determine ciprofloxacin, enrofloxacin, and lomefloxacin concentrations with linear dynamic ranges of 0.025–1mgL−1, 5–160ngL−1, and 0.01–0.8mgL−1, and detection limits of 90, 5, and 6ngL−1, respectively. Recovery rates for aqueous solutions ranged from 90% to 107%. The quantification limits for AgNP-enhanced fluorimetric methods were approximately two- to fourfold lower than established methods, presenting advantages of simplicity, sensitivity, and low cost (Yin et al., 2017). A novel and simple system using CuO NPs, hydrogen peroxide, and hydrogen carbonate was developed for the chemiluminescence detection of three pharmaceuticals: atosrvastatin, rivastigimine, and topiramate. The detection reaction was based on the luminescence response inhibition of these pharmaceuticals. Under optimized conditions, linear dynamic ranges of 0.1–100, 1–400, and 10–700μM were observed, with detection limits of 130, 86, and 1.4μM for atorsvastatin, rivastigmine, and topiramate, respectively. Accuracy analyses were performed by spiking the compounds during the preparation of the pharmaceuticals and tap water samples, with recovery rates ranging between 93% and 110% (Iranifam and Hendekhale, 2017).

For more accurate and sensitive analyses, electrochemical methods are usually employed, even if complex sample pretreatments are required. Electrochemical-based biosensors have been applied for the detection of different antibiotics, employing different types of nanomaterials and transduction mechanisms (amperometric, impedimetric, voltammetric, and photoelectrochemical). A large portion of the developed electrochemical platforms have focused on the detection of antibiotics in food samples, as covered in an excellent review by Lan et al. (2017). The photoelectrochemical detection of chloramphenicol was achieved using nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) coupled with aptamers as biological recognition elements. The large surface area and π-conjugated structure of N-GQDs ensured the facile immobilization of aptamers through π–π stacking interactions. The linear response ranged from 10 to 250nM with a detection limit of 3.1nM, and the sensor has exhibited high sensitivity, selectivity, good reproducibility, and high stability (Liu et al., 2015). Another photoelectrochemical platform for the detection of kanamycin was developed based on GO doped with water-dispersible graphite-like carbon nitride (w-g-C3N4). The chemical structure of the sensor provided aptamer bounding via π–π stacking interactions. The kanamycin molecules, captured by aptamers, resulted in an increased photocurrent. A linear response range of between 1 and 230nM was achieved, with adetection limit of 0.2nM. The sensing system displayed high selectivity, good reproducibility, and high stability (Li et al., 2014b).

Endocrine disrupting chemicals (EDCs) are substances capable of disturbing the endocrine systems of animals, causing adverse effects in aquatic organisms and the human body. As these compounds can persist in water even after purification treatments, monitoring actions are becoming a priority for environmental and regulatory agencies. Thus the development of strategies for the detection and analysis of these compounds at low concentration levels are essential.

17β-Estradiol (E2) is an endogen estrogen and one of the most important EDCs. A review approached the use of a nanomaterial-based aptamer sensor for the quantitative detection of E2 (Nezami et al., 2017). Different types of nanomaterials have been employed along with aptamers for the detection of E2 through different approaches, including optical and electrochemical. In optical systems, the conformation of aptamers changes when recognition events with target molecules occur. These conformational modifications can modulate the emission properties of fluorescent particles. Fluorescence detection was achieved for E2 by QDs, employing FRET phenomenon (Nezami et al., 2017).

A fluorescent aptasensor for the determination of E2 was developed based on AuNPs and Rhodamine B (RhoB). In the absence of an analyte, AuNPs, wrapped by the E2 aptamer, stayed dispersed in the solution, thus impairing RhoB fluorescence. When E2 was present, the combination with E2 aptamer led to AuNPs aggregation, disrupting the fluorescence quenching of RhoB. A linear range between 0.48 and 200nM was achieved, with a detection limit of 0.48nM. E2 was spiked into water samples for recovery tests and the recovering rates ranged from 94.3% to 111.7% (Ni et al., 2017). Colorimetric assays for the detection of E2 have also been developed using aptamer-coated AuNPs. 76-mer E2 aptamer was split into two short pieces and immobilized on AuNPs. The split fragments have retained their original affinity and specificity, while increasing the detection limit by 10-fold (detection limit=0.1ngmL−1) with a linear range of 450−750nM (Liu et al., 2014b). In another work, 35- and 22-mer aptamers (designed based on 76-mer aptamer) were also adhered to AuNPs. Smaller aptamers have also shown improved sensitivity, increasing the detection limit 25-fold, when compared to a full aptamer, with a linear response from 50 to 800nM (Alsager et al., 2015).

Electrochemical aptasensors have also been employed for the detection of E2 using different types of nanomaterials. Dendrimers based on a 1 poly(propylene imine)-polythiophene (G1PPT-co-PEDOT) star copolymer were used for Au electrode coating. A 76-mer aptamer was then bound onto dendrimeric film via streptavidin–biotin interaction. The nanobiosensor showed great selectivity and reproducibility for E2 detection, with a dynamic linear range between 0.1 and 100nM (Olowu et al., 2011). A dendritic gold microstructure was deposited onto a boron-doped diamond electrode for use in E2 detection. The E2 aptamer binds to the dendritic structure through Au–S interaction. The constructed aptasensor showed a wide linear range from 10fM to 1nM, with a detection limit of 5fM. The sensor showed great performance in recovering tests with environmental water samples and great selectivity when tested with molecules similar to E2 (Ke et al., 2014). A label-free electrochemical aptasensor was developed using nickel hexacyanoferrate NPs (NiHCNFe-NPs) as an in situ signal probe. AuNPs were used along with NiHCNFe-NPs, providing an immobilization platform for the aptamer and further enhanced the conductivity and stability of the signal probe. A linear range of 1–600fM was obtained with a detection limit of 0.8fM. Recovery tests on wastewater samples have shown satisfactory rates (Fan et al., 2015). Carbon nanotube field effect transistor aptasensors have been used for real-time monitoring of E2 in liquids. Through detection using a 35-mer E2 aptamer, the sensing platform showed a dynamic range of 50nM to 1.6μM (Zheng et al., 2015). Another sensing platform for the electrochemical detection of E2 has been developed by combining the aptamer probe, cobalt sulfide (CoS) nanosheets, and an AuNP-modified electrode. The CoS/AuNPs film formed on the sensor surface was able to enhance electron transfer, thus providing a dynamic concentration range of 1pM to 1nM and a detection limit of 0.7pM (Huang et al., 2015) (Table 3.2 ).

Other steroid estrogens have been targeted for detection and quantification by nanomaterials. rGO modified with antimony NPs (SbNPS) have been used for the electrochemical determination of estriol in natural water samples through oxidation reactions. The synergistic system provided adetection limit of 0.5nM (Cesarino et al., 2015). Another electrochemical detection platform was developed for the electrochemical determination of estriol in environmental and biological samples. A stable conducting film based on rGO, AuNPs, and potato starch (PS) was used for modifying glassy carbon electrodes. A linear spanning range from 1.5 to 22μM was obtained with a detection limit of 0.48μM, while the rates for recovery tests ranged from 92% to 106% (Jodar et al., 2017).

Pesticides are extensively used compounds for preventing diseases and improving crop production rates in field conditions. Organophosphorus (OP) compounds are a heterogeneous family of chemicals derived from phosphoric acid, and are the most used class of pesticide. Although, OP compounds can be rapidly degraded, their overuse causes the accumulation of OP residues in soil, crops, and wastewater runoff. OP compounds are highly toxic substances and are known to be the most toxic pesticides to vertebrate animals, causing eye irritation, abdominal pain, convulsions, respiratory system failure, and other neurological disorders. Due to their toxicity, the presence of these compounds in the environment must be monitored. OP pesticides are irreversible inhibitors of acetylcholinesterase enzyme (AChE), an essential enzyme for the synapse transmission process. Due to this property, AChE is commonly used in OP detection systems. OP hydrolase (OPH) enzymes are another type of enzyme employed in OP detection platforms. Numerous OP sensors have been developed based on nanomaterials, like carbon nanotubes, metallic NPs, graphene-based composites, quantum dots, nanocrystal coordination polymers, and magnetic NPs (Kaur and Prabhakar, 2017). Nanomaterials act as multifunctional platforms, provide immobilization for biomolecules, and accelerate electronic transfer, resulting in increased sensitivity, selectivity, and accuracy for the detection of OP. Carbon nanotubes and graphene-based materials provide a greater interfacial contact area between analyte and transducer, thereby enhancing electron transfer kinetics (Dhull et al., 2015; Hassani et al., 2017; Kaur and Prabhakar, 2017). Various NPs have been exploited for the electrochemical detection of OP compounds. Some NPs can interact directly with functional groups present on the OP structure (through affinity with the sulfur/phosphorous group or via π–π stacking interactions). The bond between NPs and OP results in the inhibition reduction of NPs, resulting in a change in electrochemical signal (Kaur and Prabhakar, 2017).

CdTe QDs and AuNPs were used along with AchE immobilized on chitosan microspheres for the electrochemical detection of monocrotophos (Du et al., 2008). The QDs and AuNPs promoted a synergistic effect, enhancing electronic transfers and catalyzing electrooxidation, thus improving the detection sensitivity. The CdTe QDs also prevented the leaching of AChE molecules through covalent binding via Schiff bases. Detection was achieved at two linear ranges: 1–1000ngmL−1 and 2–15μgmL−1, and the biosensor platform showed good precision, reproducibility, acceptable stability, and accuracy in the recovery tests for garlic samples. AuNPs with covalent-immobilized methyl parathion hydrolase (an OP hydrolase) on their surfaces were employed for OP detection (Fig. 3.11 ). The use of these particles coupled with a glass carbon electrode allowed for the electrochemical detection of methyl parathion at a linear range of 0.2–100ngmL−1 with a detection limit of 0.07ngmL−1. This sensing platform showed high sensitivity, specificity, reproducibility, and stability with satisfying recovery rates (Liu et al., 2014a). Zirconia NPs were deposited onto gold electrodes for the detection of methyl parathion. The strong affinity between zirconia and the phosphate group of the OP compound allowed for binding to the NP surface. The electrochemical detection system presented a linear behavior of between 5 and 100ngmL−1 with a detection limit of 1ngmL−1, also showing good precision (Liu and Lin, 2005). Graphene nanosheets decorated with zirconia NPs (ZrO2NPs-GNs) were synthetized onto a cathodic substrate and used for the electrochemical detection of methyl parathion. The combined properties of the ZrO2 NPs (high recognition capability of phosphoric moieties) and GNs (large surface area and high conductivity) highly enhanced the sensitivity of the composite nanosensor, providing a detection limit of 0.6ngmL−1 (Gong et al., 2012).

Due to the increasing worldwide concern regarding homeland security and human and environmental welfare, the detection of explosive compounds has gained great importance in counterterrorism actions and for the prevention of soil and water contamination. Besides their explosive effect, explosives can have harmful effects on biological systems, causing toxic and mutagenic effects as some compounds can rapidly penetrate the skin (Akhgari et al., 2015; Ma et al., 2015). Nitroaromatic-based and nitroamine-based explosives are the most prevalent in military use. These chemicals are composed of a benzene ring functionalized with nitro or methyl groups. Some highly developed techniques are already used for the detection of explosives, such as liquid and gas chromatography mass spectrometry (LC/GC-MS), Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectrometry, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS), ion-mobility spectrometry, enzymatic assays, optodes, and electrochemistry. However, they are mostly limited by the need for sophisticated equipment and time-consuming processes, thus making these techniques unsuitable for in-field and real-time analyses. Considering these limitations, the development of nanosensors for overcoming these issues and providing reliable methods for the rapid and sensitive determination of these compounds is required. For their ability to enhance physical and chemical properties, nanomaterials have been applied in the design of nanosensors for the detection of nitroaromatic explosives (Akhgari et al., 2015; Ma et al., 2015). QDs, due to their unique optoelectronic properties, are the most used type of nanomaterial in optical detection systems for nitroaromatic explosives (Akhgari et al., 2015). Due to the electron-withdrawing nitro groups in the benzene ring, nitroaromatics stand as good acceptors of electrons. For this reason, they can act as quenchers for electron-rich luminescent nanomaterials via PET or FRET (Akhgari et al., 2015; Ma et al., 2015).

Blue luminescent graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were developed for the quantification of trinitrophenol (TNP). The GQDs were derived from citric acid and emit a strong blue florescence at 450nm after excitation at 365nm. TNP interacts with the GCDs through π–π stacking interactions, resulting in fluorescence quenching by FRET. The decrease in fluorescence was linearly proportional to the TNP concentrations of between 0.1 and 15μmolL−1, with a detection limit of 0.091μmolL−1. The method exhibited high sensitivity, good linearity, and reproducibility, with the capacity to be directly applied to real water samples (Li et al., 2015). A sensing system for TNT was designed by employing gold nanorods (AuNRs) and CdTe QDs. The AuNRs and CdTe QDs form a compact hybrid through electrostatic amine–carboxyl interactions, leading to fluorescence quenching by FRET phenomena (Fig. 3.12 ). The TNT molecules could covalently bind to primary amines present on the AuNRs, releasing QDs from the interaction with the AuNRs (quenchers). Thus FRET is switched off and near-infrared fluorescence is turned on. The system showed a detection limit of 0.1nM, a linear range between 1.1 and 66nM, and excellent selectivity, and it was successfully applied for TNT sensing in environmental samples (Xia et al., 2011). Methionine functionalized-CdTe QDs and CdTe/ZnS core–shell QDs were used for the fluorescent detection of different nitroaromatic explosives: dinitrotoluene (DNT), nitrotoluene (NT), and nitrobenzene (NB). Nitroaromatic explosive molecules covalently bind to functionalized QDs, resulting in fluorescence quenching via electronic transfer from the QDs to the explosive molecules. The surface passivation and inductive effects presented by methionine functionalized CdTe/ZnS core–shell QDs contributed to enhancing the sensitivity of the system (Pazhanivel et al., 2013).
