Carbon nanotubes and graphene constitute the second most applied group of nanomaterials for the detection of nitroaromatic explosive compounds. Their fast conductivity, high elasticity, high mechanical strength, and high surface area properties can be helpful in both electrochemical and optical sensors (Akhgari et al., 2015). Goh and Pumera tested different systems containing single-, few-, and multilayered graphene nanoribbons and graphite microparticles for the electrochemical detection of TNT in seawater. The detection was based on the adhesion of TNT via π–π stacking interactions and electrochemical reduction. The authors identified no significant differences between the different systems, establishing a detection limit of 1μgmL−1 (Goh and Pumera, 2011). A nanocomposite containing Cu NPs and SWCNTs was used for modifying glassy carbon electrodes applied in the electrochemical detection of TNT. The adsorptive properties of SWCNTs as well as their enhanced electroactive surface area and electrical conductivity contributed to the achievement of a detection limit of 4.4nM, with a linear range reaching 4.4μM. TNT could be detected by this sensing platform in different samples, such as river water, tap water, and soil samples (Hrapovic et al., 2006).

Metallic NPs have also been used in both types of detection systems due to their catalytic, electrical, and optical effects (Akhgari et al., 2015). Cysteamine-stabilized AuNPs were used for the colorimetric detection of TNT. The nitroaromatic explosive reacted with the primary amines on the surface of the AuNPs, leading to aggregation and subsequently to a color change, which was easily observed by the naked eye. A linear range between 0.5pM and 5nM was achieved, with a detection limit of 7 and 4nM, for lake water and soil samples, respectively (Jiang et al., 2008). Amine-capped ZnS-Mn2+ nanocrystals were used for the fluorescence detection of TNT. The amine caps interact with the nitroaromatic analytes through acid–base pairing interactions between electron-rich amino ligands and electron-deficient aromatic ligands, and this interaction results in fluorescence quenching by electronic transfer. This sensing system showed a linear range of 10–100μM, with a detection limit of 1nM, but moderate selectivity was observed under the presence of other nitroaromatic compounds (Tu et al., 2008). Modified AuNPs were embedded on Au electrodes for the electrochemical detection of TNT. AuNP films were incorporated into the electrodes through cross-linking with three different molecules: p-aminothiophenolate, oligothioaniline, and oligoaniline. The AuNP films bridged by oligoaniline achieved the best performance, with increased sensitivity by a factor of 135 when compared to the bare Au electrode, corresponding to a detection limit of 200pM. The synergistic effects of the oligoaniline and AuNP matrix as π-donor sites enhanced the π-donor–acceptor interactions with TNT, contributing to the improved sensitivity (Riskin et al., 2008). For sensors applied in the detection of nitroaromatic-based explosives, optical platforms presented lower detection limits, but shorter detection ranges. Electrochemical sensors, however, have been designed with lower instrumental costs (Akhgari et al., 2015).

It is clear that water, even being one of the simplest substances of the nature, is an essential compound to keep the life on earth. However, the history displays that the contamination level, as well as the number of water contaminants has increased over the time (1900 until now), which is caused by the human being. On the other hand, the technology to sense organic and/or inorganic entities in water has greatly advanced in the past few decades. Since many nanostructures were developed regarding the detection of such contaminants like heavy metal ions and antibiotics, for example. Although, the use of the nanomaterials must be carefully applied because the nanoparticles in water may lead to unknown consequences in the environmental. Therefore taking into consideration the exposed, the nanotechnology has greatly increased the number of strategies regarding the sense of water contaminants. However the development of better devices to lower the detection limit of a given analyte must continue. Additionally, it is worth to mention that the concern regarding the emerging contaminants like nanomaterials, for example. For these kinds of entities, protocols for in situ monitoring are still a challenge. Thus it is easy to see that the work in this field is an ongoing mission.
